sections.t: Introduction — scope, governing legislation, source materials
sections.h:
INTRODUCTION

This manual contains the overarching principles relating to the execution of search warrants by the WA Police Force. Any police officer can execute a search warrant, whether it was applied for by them or another officer1.

Every member in charge of a warrant and every member involved in a warrant is individually responsible for their actions. This manual is an informing document that provides guidance for members in charge of a search warrant, supervisors and staff at all stages of development who seek to learn about search warrants.

Reference material relating to WA Police Force search warrants is located in the following areas:

• Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA) (CIA) s.41-45.

• Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 (WA) - (MDA) s.24.

• Firearms Act 1973 (WA) - (FA) s. 26.

• Weapons Act 1999 (WA) – (W/Act) s.14.

• Police Manual SZ-01.03 Search Warrants.

• Body Worn Camera (BWC) SOPs 8.2 Use of BWC During Search Warrants.

This document principally refers to the procedures required to comply with the CIA. Where specific legislation relates to MDA, FA or W/Act warrants, reference is made to the different procedures and relevant statute. Although many similarities exist when executing all types of warrants, members must know the legislative differences that apply in the relevant acts.

Warrant templates can be located:

• The WA Police Force CIA Forms intranet page, located here.

• The Investigative Standards intranet page under the heading, Search Warrants.

• Relevant legislation.

CONSIDERATIONS PRIOR TO EXECUTING A

SEARCH WARRANT

sections.t: Considerations prior — Obtaining the Warrant
sections.h:
Obtaining the Warrant

Application for search warrants must meet legal requirements for the valid issue of the warrant. The Search Warrant Drafting Guide provides instructions that align with the legal requirements.

For approval relating to warrants for unoccupied premises and conditions for an independent person, refer to the unoccupied premises section, within this document.

sections.t: Considerations prior — Planning and Responsibilities (Operational Order, MIC, PMO, VCO, Min Officers)
sections.h:
Planning and Responsibilities

Operational Order

In every case where members execute a search warrant, the member in charge shall ensure that an investigative operational order is prepared. The order will contain:

• A risk assessment of the premises and occupants.

• A statement of the roles and responsibilities for the:

▪ Member-in-charge of search

▪ Interviewing officer

▪ Physical material officer

▪ Video camera operator

▪ Searching officer

▪ Security officer

• A record of the briefing provided by the member in charge of the warrant or senior

investigating officer.

Member in Charge of Search

The member in charge of the search will ensure:

• The warrant is reviewed for correctness and is signed by a Justice of the Peace (JP).

• They are in possession of the warrant at the time of execution, unless the warrant is

obtained by remote communication.

• All members of the search warrant have reviewed the operational order, are aware of their

responsibilities and are aware of potential risks.

Physical Material Officer

The physical material officer will ensure a forensic exhibits kit2is provided at each warrant.

Video Camera Officer

The video camera operator will ensure an approved electronic device used to audio-visually record the planned search warrant is charged and in working order.

Minimum Number of Officers

Six officers is the recommended minimum to conduct a safe, efficient and effective search.3

2 Refer to Forensic exhibit handling and packaging guidelines.

3 This does not preclude an officer in charge of a search from deploying fewer officers; for example, when executing a low risk search warrant to seize CCTV footage etc. The member in charge of the warrant will continually assess the risk, ensuring sufficient staff are provided to mitigate the risk.

sections.t: Considerations prior — Planned Time of Execution
sections.h:
Planned Time of Execution

The warrant is to be executed at a time appropriate to the legislation. Under s.43(6) CIA, a search warrant is to be executed between 6:00am and 9:00pm, unless the officer executing it reasonably suspects that if it were, the safety of any person, including the officer, may be endangered or the effectiveness of the proposed search may be jeopardised.

Under s.24(1) MDA, a search warrant can be executed at any time, however it is recommended all search warrants are executed between 6:00am and 9:00pm unless for the reasons above.

ENTRY PROCEDURE

The execution of all search warrants and searches will be audio visually recorded4.

All members are to ensure searches are conducted in a thorough and professional manner and the integrity of the search is maintained at all times.

In general, search warrants may be executed by:

• Knocking and announcing. Planned lower risk warrant where it is planned to approach

the target place, and seek occupiers consent to enter by knocking on the door and talking to an occupant5.

• Tactical forced entry. Planned higher risk warrant where rapid tactical entry is

reasonably believed to be necessary, and occupiers rights are given to the occupant after an initial security search of the target place is complete. The policy for tactical forced entry is located at FR-01.08 Forced Entry to Premises.

A third type of warrant, a covert entry warrant is discussed later in this manual.

sections.t: Entry — Body Worn Cameras
sections.h:
Body Worn Cameras - Entry

BWCs are to be turned on and recording prior to entering a target place6, until after occupier’s rights7 are given and the target place is secured.

After police enter and secure the target place, the member in charge of the search will coordinate the turning on of the audiovisual recording device and switching BWC to buffering mode.8

sections.t: Entry — Occupier's Rights s.31(2) CIA
sections.h:
Occupier’s Rights s.31(2) CIA

CIA occupier’s rights are an essential consideration of any warrant. The officer in charge must consider the requirements of the legislation under which the warrant was issued. For example:

• ‘Occupier’s Rights’, s.31 CIA are to be provided to all occupants in CIA search warrants.

Rights will generally be given before entry unless the member in charge of the search reasonably suspects that to do so will endanger any person, including the officer, or jeopardise the purpose of the proposed entry and or the effectiveness of any search of the place9.

• In MDA Warrants there is no legislative requirement to provide occupiers rights found at

s.31 CIA. It is recommended that where practicable, the same rights outlined in s.31 CIA are given to occupiers where an MDA warrant is executed. This recommendation does not apply to MDA warrants where covert entry is made. The decision and rationale to provide or not to provide occupier’s rights rests with the member in charge of the MDA warrant.

sections.t: Entry — Knock and Announce
sections.h:
Knock and Announce Entry

BWC is to remain activated until occupier’s consent is obtained and the premises is secured10.

Officers will11:

• Identify themselves to the occupier. If in plain clothes, produce police identification.

• Inform the occupier that it is intended to enter the place.

• If the place is to be entered under a search warrant, give the occupier a copy of the

warrant.

• If the place is to be entered under some other statutory authority, inform the occupier of

the reason, and the statutory authority, for the entry

• Give the occupier an opportunity to give informed consent to the place being entered.

Occupier’s consent only extends to allowing police to enter the premises. The CIA search warrant gives police the power to both enter and search the premises. Regardless of occupier’s consent, police will execute the search warrant.

sections.t: Entry — Use of Force
sections.h:
Use of Force

If entry is refused, reasonable force may be used to gain entry12.
sections.b: Use of Force If entry is refused, reasonable force may be used to gain entry12.

sections.t: Entry — Security
sections.h:
Security

Once entry is made, secure the target premises and assemble those present in a suitable area. Safety and security of police, suspects, witnesses and members of the public is to be a primary consideration at all times.

sections.t: Entry — Tactical Forced Entry
sections.h:
Tactical Forced Entry

A tactical forced entry may be necessary when an officer reasonably suspects that if they were to enter in compliance with s.31(2) CIA (Occupier's Rights), such entry will endanger any person and or may jeopardise the proposed entry or effectiveness of the search.

Where an officer intends to conduct a tactical entry, they must be justified in doing so. For all search warrants, officers are encouraged to consult their supervisor or an independent senior officer prior to executing a tactical entry. A “Forced Entry Risk Assessment” form must be completed where it is known a forced entry will occur.

Before consideration of forced entry is made to premises, the member in charge shall ensure:

• The correct target address is confirmed.

• The necessity to use force is considered.

• The amount of planned force is reasonable.

• The vulnerabilities and age (especially children) of all occupants is considered.

• All members are dressed in contemporary personal protective equipment as prescribed

by Operational Safety & Tactics Training Unit (OSTTU).

• Damage is kept to the minimum required to affect the entry.

• The necessity for Senior Officer Orders for adjacent premises is considered.

In cases of high risk, officers may request the Tactical Response Group (TRG) to assist in the entry. Refer to High Risk Entry and SS-7.2.2 TRG Call-Out and Deployment Procedures.

• Upon arrival operate BWC in accordance with BWC SOP 8.2.

• Enter the premises in accordance with Method of Entry procedures available from OSTTU,

using approved method of entry equipment available on RMIS.

• Once entry is made, secure the premises and assemble those present in a suitable area.

• As soon as possible after police have entered and secured the target place, provide

occupiers rights to the occupant.

ARRESTED SUSPECT AND ACCOMPANYING

PERSONS RIGHTS

After entry is made and the target place is secured, occupier’s rights are given and occupants are secured in a suitable area, the legal status of all occupants must now be considered.

Obtain personal details from each occupant and decide their status.

sections.t: Arrested Suspect rights — s.128 / s.137 / s.138 CIA
sections.h:
Arrested Suspect – s.128 CIA

Ensure the Arrested Suspect is provided their CIA s.137 and s.138 rights.

Record the time and place the rights were given. Ensure the arrested suspect is cautioned and has a full understanding of each section of the police caution and their rights.
sections.b: Arrested Suspect – s.128 CIA Ensure the Arrested Suspect is provided their CIA s.137 and s.138 rights. Record the time and place the rights were given. Ensure the arrested suspect is cautioned and has a full understanding of each section of the police caution and their rights.

sections.t: Accompanying Person rights — s.28 CIA
sections.h:
Accompanying Person – s.28 CIA

People accompanying officers to be informed of their rights – s.28 CIA. This applies to all search warrants regardless of the legislative power.

Record the time and place the rights were given. Ensure the accompanying person is cautioned and has a full understanding of each section of the police caution and their rights.

If there is any uncertainty whether the accompanying person may become a suspect, provide all of their rights at s.137 and s.138 CIA, with the exception of telling them they are under arrest. This will safeguard any subsequent confessional evidence.

sections.t: Detain and Search Persons Present — s.43(8)(b)(ii) CIA / s.24(1) MDA
sections.h:
Detain and Search Persons Present

s.43(8)(b)(ii) CIA authorises an officer to conduct a basic or strip search of a person who is in the target place when the warrant is being executed for the target thing.

s.24(1) MDA specifies that any person present can be detained and searched during a search warrant, using such force as necessary.

If necessary to search an occupant of the premises the video camera is to remain on during the course of the body search. In the case of a strip search the camera is to be pointed away so as not to visually record the body search.

The officer conducting the search is to provide an audio commentary of what is occurring so as to allow the audio to be recorded. If any items of interest are located they may be photographed in the position they are found on the body.

sections.t: Searching — Member in Charge of Search responsibilities (during search)
sections.h:
Member in Charge of Search

The member in charge of the warrant is expected to have sufficient experience or supervision to lead a search warrant. The member in charge of the search should oversee the search and not involve themselves in physically searching the target place. They will exercise overarching control of the search, delegating tasks where appropriate and ensuring the lawful collection of evidence. The member in charge of the search will take all necessary steps to minimise the risk of harm to all people present.

After entry the member in charge will consider:

• Inviting the occupant to declare any prohibited or dangerous items, or those items

prescribed in the warrant.

• Ensure where possible that the occupant views the search14.

13 s.65(4) CIA

14 s.31 CIA• If the occupant is not present or declines to accompany police, the member in charge of the search will stop the search and comply with the process outlined in the unoccupied premises and independent person section.

• Do not place the occupant or independent person in a position where they must observe

police search more than one room (or area) at a time15.

• Conduct a systematic (room to room, one room at a time) search of the target place.

• Where a room or area is locked, or access is made difficult because the occupant has

secured a room, request the occupier’s assistance to access the room (keys, code) before using force to gain entry.

• Prior to stopping an audiovisual recording ensure a time and date is announced.

• When an audiovisual recording is recommenced, ensure a time and date is announced,

and provide a short commentary on what occurred whilst the camera was switched off.

Invite the occupier to comment. Remind the occupier of their rights and the police caution.

• Sign and date the execution of the search warrant on both the occupiers copy and the

original.

• File the endorsed warrant with the application on the CRIME file.

• Ensure you retain possession of the original warrant so it can be produced in court if

required.

• Ensure that forced entry repairs or damage to any property is dealt with appropriately.

sections.t: Searching — Audiovisual Recording of Search Warrants
sections.h:
Audiovisual Recording of Search Warrants

s.45(2) CIA states that if reasonably practicable, an audiovisual recording must be made of the execution of the search warrant. Officers will audio visually record all search warrants, unless exceptional circumstances exist. Where a recording is not made, officers must be prepared to justify the absence of that recording to the court.

Generally, it is not practical to video record an entire search warrant, therefore intermittent recording should be adopted. Where intermittent video recording is utilised, on each occasion recording is re-commenced, the officer in charge will give a short commentary on what occurred whilst the camera was switched off and invite the occupier to comment.

Where a search is not audio visually recorded, the officer entering the execution of a search warrant on IMS will briefly outline the reasons for the absence of any audiovisual recording of the search in the free form text area of that system.

All members participating in the search are to be mindful of inappropriate language or comments made during search warrants. Poor language, comments or behaviour by police can unnecessarily prejudice the admissibility of evidence. Officers are to take great care and remain professional at all times.

All search warrant recordings are copied to disc and placed onto IMS property as a Search Warrant Recording, including search warrants recorded on One Force mobile phones. This

sections.t: Searching — Video Camera Operator duties and AV checklist
sections.h:
Video Camera Operator

The nominated camera operator will record the warrant. The camera operator's duties include:

• Ensuring the audiovisual equipment is functional before deploying to the search location.

• Ensuring the time and date showing on the video camera is correct.

• Ensuring the time and date is recorded. If the time and date is not correct or is not

functioning, consider changing the video camera. If this is not a viable option explanation of the incorrect date and time should be stated on the recording at the earliest opportunity.

• The camera operator will record the current condition of the target premises by conducting

a pan of the premises before searching commences, and at the conclusion of the search, in company with the occupier and security officer.

These procedures outline the minimum requirement for the recording of searches. Where deemed appropriate and practicable, additional parts of the search or the entire search may be recorded. The audiovisual recording of the search shall include:

• An introduction of all members assisting during the search warrant to the occupier.

• A commentary of all occupier’s rights and introductions provided.

• The police caution and check of understanding.

• The production of the search warrant.

• A request to declare any prohibited items such as prohibited drugs.

• A request to declare any dangerous items secured or unsecured that pose a potential

occupational health and safety risk to occupiers or police involved such as hypodermic syringes.

• A request to declare any weapons secured or unsecured such as firearms.

• A request to declare any large sums of money or items of value such as jewellery or gold.

• A request to declare any items particularised in the warrant.

• An explanation to the occupier of the procedure to be adopted during the search.

• Any items located during the search, which are to be seized.

• The counting of any money located during the search.

• The sealing into Security Movement Envelopes (SMEs) of any money, items of value or

drugs.

• Any comment made by the occupier relating to items located during the search.

• The process where the physical material officer identifies and shows the items seized to

the occupant, providing them with an Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log (Copy), including the return of any items to the occupier.

• Any damage caused during the entry to the premises or the subsequent search.

• Any complaint made by the occupier.

• While recording, if a recording is interrupted for any technical reason (battery, fault), upon

resumption of recording an explanation is to be recorded.

sections.t: Searching — Searching Officer duties
sections.h:
Searching Officer

Great care is to be taken when searching for evidence. Experience has shown when searches are rushed or not systematic, evidence can be overlooked. Personal safety is to be exercised when searching and members should take every opportunity to use appropriate personal protective equipment.

Searchers will employ a mindset that considers the protection of all forensic evidence in accordance with forensic exhibit handling procedures. If physical material is located and a video recording device is not on, if the officer is wearing BWC they should immediately activate their BWC, back capturing the previous 30 seconds, including the moments when they discovered the item. The member in charge of the search will coordinate the switching of BWC to buffering mode once the video camera operator recommences recording.

sections.t: Searching — Physical Material Officer duties (during search)
sections.h:
Physical Material Officer

• Direct the video camera operator to video or photograph seized property in situ prior to

moving the item. This is particularly important if the weight or nature of an exhibit is likely to make production in court impractical.

• Packaging of seized items will comply with Forensic Exhibit Handling and Packaging

Guidelines.

• Every item seized is to be recorded on the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log,

noting the location of where it was found. Where possible use the description of the room as specified by the occupant in the initial pan of the premises.

• Where no property or drugs are seized the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log

shall be endorsed “Nil Seized”.

• Ensure that seized money, items of value or drugs are recorded.

• SMEs are used to secure seized money, items of value or drugs.

• At completion of the search, the physical material officer will:

▪ Review the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log.

▪ Identify items located during the search which are to be seized.

▪ Return any items not to be seized to the occupier.

• A copy of the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log is to be provided to the

occupier. Invite the occupier to sign the log as a true and accurate record of the items seized.

sections.t: Securing the Premises After the Search
sections.h:
Securing the Premises After the Search

At the completion of a search, where no person is to remain at the premises, the member in charge shall ensure the premises are left secure.

If the premises are unable to be secured, arrangements shall be made for a contractor to attend and affect the necessary repairs prior to police departure. Where damage has occurred effecting entry or during the search, such damage shall be photographed or audio visually recorded.

sections.t: Forced Entry Repairs — DOS / RWA process & payment criteria
sections.h:
Forced Entry Repairs

At the completion of a forced entry search, where no person remains at the premises, the member in charge of the search must ensure that the premises are left secured. The following procedure will assist officers when securing premises:

• If the premises are unable to be secured, arrangements should be made in the

metropolitan area through the District Operational Supervisor (DOS) or in Regional WA (RWA), the relevant District Office for a service provider to attend and affect the necessary repairs prior to police departure.

• Upon receiving the following information, DOS or RWA equivalent will arrange for the

service provider to attend. The requesting officer will provide:

▪ Name, rank and regimental number.

▪ Station/unit.

▪ CAD incident job number.

▪ Nature of the damage.

• Payment by WA Police for forced entry repairs shall only be authorised where:

▪ Temporary repairs are required in order to secure premises.

▪ Unnecessary or unreasonable force was used to gain entry.

▪ No offender or evidence was found and no charges result from the search.

▪ An innocent third party is financially disadvantaged as result of damage caused on

entry or during the search.

PROPERTY GENERALLY

sections.t: Property Generally — Seizure of Property (process, storage, IMS, transfer to EMU)
sections.h:
Seizure of Property

• All seized property is to be recorded onto an Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material

Log.

• The original Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log (white copy) is to be retained

with the CRIME file/brief, the duplicate copy (pink) is to be given to the occupier and the triplicate copy (yellow) is to remain in the book for Good Governance.

• Property details recorded on the Property Receipt/Physical Material Log are to be entered

onto IMS as soon as practicable17.

• All seized property shall be correctly labelled and include the IMS number and IMS

barcode.

• All seized property shall be stored securely and correctly prior to transfer to the Exhibit

Management Unit (EMU). Seized property is not to be stored in desks, filing cabinets or the general office areas of your working area.

• All movements of seized property shall be recorded on IMS.

• At the completion of the court hearing, all seized property shall be dealt with in accordance

with the court order.

• All receipts for the return of property shall be filed with the officer-in-charge.

17 PR-1.1.3 Recording and Entering Property in Police Possession

sections.t: Property Generally — Seizure of Drugs
sections.h:
Seizure of Drugs

• All drug seizures shall be sealed in SMEs or a vented bag and secured with a security tag.

• The securing of the prohibited drugs into the SME is to be audio visually recorded.

• The SME shall be marked with a description of the seizure and the signature and

regimental number of the exhibit officer and correctly recorded onto the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log.

• The occupier or an independent person shall be requested to sign the SME.

• Subsequent weighing of drugs at a police station will be done in accordance PR-01.02.

All seized drug (and paraphernalia) property shall be correctly entered on IMS.

‘Clan Lab’ responses are located in the Clandestine Drug Laboratories section of this document.

sections.t: Property Generally — Seizure of Money
sections.h:
Seizure of Money

• Audio visually record and photograph in situ any money located.

• In the presence of the occupier, count the money and place it into an SME.

• If the volume of money is substantial, package the money into an SME and use the ‘money

serial recorder’ located at the Drug & Firearm Squad.

• If the volume of money presents a handling problem, additional envelopes shall be

obtained or a commissioned officer may arrange for another secure method of transport.

• The occupier shall be requested to read aloud the envelope numbers.

• All seized money shall be correctly entered onto IMS.

• All SMEs containing money shall be stored in a safe or another secure location designated

for that purpose.

• All seized money shall be banked in the Police Suspense Account unless it is required for

some legitimate forensic purpose. The receipt shall be filed with the officer-in-charge.

• Prior to banking any seized money or dealing with it in any way it shall be photographed

in a manner to record type, denominations and consideration should be given to recording individual serial numbers.

• When any money is banked, IMS shall be updated.

sections.t: Property Generally — Opening Security Movement Envelopes (SMEs)
sections.h:
Opening Security Movement Envelopes (SMEs)

• When SMEs containing drugs or other items seized are opened for any reason, the whole

procedure shall be audiovisually recorded in the presence of two officers.

• The SME shall be cut open at the bottom, so that the seals are left intact. The member

cutting open the SME shall endorse the time, date and location of opening the envelope and sign it. A witnessing member shall also sign the envelope. The reason for the opening the envelope should be audio visually recorded.

• Upon re-sealing the contents into a new SME, the opened envelope shall also be sealed

in the new envelope in a manner so that the serial number is clearly visible.

• Both officers are to sign the new SME.• Record the number of the second SME on the item record within the IMS, together with

the date and time of opening, the reasons for opening, and the names of the authorising supervisor, the officer opening the SME, and the witnessing officer.

• Audiovisual recordings used to record the opening of SMEs and the processing of physical

material shall be stored at the recording office/station.

• For further information, refer PR-01.02.1.1.

OTHER SEARCH WARRANT CONSIDERATIONS

sections.t: Other — Unoccupied Premises and Independent Person Considerations (and Guide)
sections.h:
Unoccupied Premises and Independent Person

Considerations

When entry is affected and the premises are found to be unoccupied, the member in charge shall:

• Immediately withdraw all staff from the premises.

• Advise a commissioned officer who may nominate an independent person (sergeant or

above) to be present during the search.

• Ensure no search is conducted until the arrival the nominated independent person.

• No approval shall be granted unless it is shown that it is necessary to conduct the search

in the absence of the occupier or owner.

• Where the premises are unoccupied the member in charge must leave the following in a

prominent position:

▪ A notice stating the officer’s official details and that the place has been entered.

▪ If entry was gained by search warrant, a copy of that warrant.

▪ A copy of the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log.

▪ If entry was obtained by the use of another statutory authority, the reason, and the

statutory authority, for the entry.

Independent Person Guide

Independent persons are appointed to ensure and observe compliance with search warrant procedures. They are to ensure impartiality. They will not make operational decisions or have specific involvement in the search or seizure. Should a situation arise that requires intervention from the independent person, they shall bring this matter to the attention of the member in charge of the search.

Independent persons must:

• Be nominated by an authorising commissioned officer.

• Hold the rank of sergeant or above (includes a member officially acting in that position or

rank).

• Where practicable, not be from the case officer’s organisational unit.

• Ensure the search warrant is sighted and correct.

• Be present throughout the entirety of the search.

• Monitor compliance and ensure the search is conducted in a professional manner.Upon arrival, and at commencement of their role, the following statements should be used as

a guide, and is to be audiovisually recorded before recommencement of the search:

“I am a nominated Supervisor, from [business unit], appointed by [Name of Authorised Commissioned Officer], as the independent person for the execution of this search warrant. My role is to monitor compliance with regards to Legislation, WA Police Force Policy and Standard Operating Procedures. I have sighted the search warrant which has been approved by a Justice of the Peace, and will ensure any physical material seized during the execution of the search warrant is recorded onto an Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log.”

sections.t: Other — Investigative Interviewing at search warrant location
sections.h:
Investigative Interviewing

All interviews, whether recorded at the search warrant location, police station or other location are to be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Force investigative interviewing model.

A preliminary interview of a suspect at the location does not negate the need for an audiovisual record of interview upon return to the police station.

A preliminary interview of a suspect at the location does not negate the need for an audiovisual record of interview upon return to the police station.

sections.t: Other — Clandestine Drug Laboratories
sections.h:
Clandestine Drug Laboratories

In the event of discovery of a clandestine drug laboratory, the member in charge of the warrant will cease all activity and withdraw all personnel to a safe distance18. Initial action includes:

• All Police personnel and suspects depart the site and remain at a safe distance in case of

fire, explosion and/or chemical contamination.

• No ignition sources are taken into the site.

• The scene will be preserved and Drug & Firearm Squad are to be notified immediately.

• No attempt is made to shut down any on-going chemical process or remove a power

source to the premises without the advice of a Forensic Chemist.

• Where possible provide the Forensic Chemist with a description of the laboratory set-up,

including whether the laboratory appears to be operating at the time.

• Do not re-enter a laboratory for any of the above purposes.

• After the Forensic Chemist has rendered the site safe, photographs and audiovisual

recordings of the scene may be made, where appropriate.

sections.t: Other — Proceeds of Crime Considerations
sections.h:
Proceeds of Crime Considerations

The Proceeds of Crime Squad (PCS) conduct investigations into matters pursuant to the Criminal Property Confiscation Act 2000 (WA) (CPCA) and may commence confiscation proceedings where:

18 CR-07.05 Clandestine Drug Laboratories• A person has been charged with a serious drug offence and has the potential to be declared a drug trafficker pursuant to s.32A(1) of the MDA.

• A person has property directly or indirectly used in or derived from a confiscable offence

(i.e. punishable by two or more years imprisonment) including being in possession of large amounts of unexplained cash.

• The apparent wealth of individuals or criminal organisations cannot be adequately

explained.

PCS can assist during the planning stage of a search warrant by compiling or providing financial / asset profiles of the target and should be contacted in all instances at the earliest opportunity if there is any potential the target person may be charged with:

• A serious drug offence likely to render them a drug trafficker under the MDA; or

• In any other Act, where property is crime used (i.e. a house used for cannabis

cultivation or a vehicle used in a sex assault) or crime derived (i.e. paid for with the proceeds of crime).

If, during a search warrant, large amounts of cash or assets reasonably suspected to be crime used or crime derived are located, contact PCS. PCS will provide advice and may attend the location. The powers under the CPCA are “here and now” powers and must be executed while the target is in possession of the property, during the search warrant. Charging the suspect prior to PCS conducting a compulsive interview will likely prevent a compulsive interview being conducted and result in potential loss of evidence.

For more information on PCS guidelines refer to the Proceeds of Crime Investigation Manual located on the Investigative Standards intranet site.

sections.t: Other — High Risk / Terrorist Incident
sections.h:
High Risk/Terrorist Incident

The deployment of TRG for all other incidents should fall within the roles and responsibilities of the TRG as outlined in SS-07.02 and will be at the discretion of the OIC TRG or the Police Tactical Group (PTG) Commander.

All personnel requesting the TRG for planned operations shall submit a Tasking Request Form and Investigators Target Summary as located in the WAPOL Intranet under the SID T&CG Process. Requests must be approved at District Inspector or Divisional Inspector level prior to being forwarded to the TRG.

The TRG will respond to all incidents classified high-risk as determined by the provisions of SS-07.02 Tactical Response Group (TRG).

sections.t: Other — Use of Detection Dogs
sections.h:
Use of Detection Dogs

• The Canine Section is able to provide the following specialist resources:

▪ Narcotic detection dogs (NDDs) for the searching of premises, vehicles, vessels etc.

▪ Passive alert drug detection dogs (PADs) for the mass screening of passengers on

public transport, patrons in entertainment districts and at special events.

• Where practical, a Tasking Request Form (TRF) is to be submitted to the Canine

Operations Deployment Supervisor. Dynamic requests will be accepted by telephone or by Station Mail email.

• The Canine Section can be contacted 24/7 via 9370 7368 and all out of hours requests

must be submitted to SOCC.

• Under no circumstances are WA Police Force members to approach other agencies such

as the Australian Border Force (ABF), Australian Federal Police (AFP) or the Department of Justice (DOJ). Protocols have been established between these external agencies to utilise all resources appropriately. These agencies will only consider the use of their dog resources at the request of the OIC Canine Section.

• After entry has been effected, and before searching commences, the dog handler shall

instruct the occupier of the procedure to be adopted during the search.

• Before searching commences the handler and the dog may examine the premises in the

presence of the occupier or independent person and a nominated member of the search team.

• Any positive reaction shown by the dog shall be brought to the attention of the member in

charge of the search before the actual search is conducted.

• The area or object of positive reaction shall be searched in the presence of the occupier

or an independent person in accordance with these procedures.

sections.t: CIA Search Warrant Ancillary Powers — s.44 CIA
sections.h:
CIA Search Warrant Ancillary Powers

A search warrant issued under the provisions of the CIA also authorises the officer executing it to exercise any or all of the ancillary powers found at s.44 CIA. Officers are required to be fully conversant with the powers conferred under a search warrant within the relevant Act prior to executing the search warrant.
sections.b: CIA Search Warrant Ancillary Powers A search warrant issued under the provisions of the CIA also authorises the officer executing it to exercise any or all of the ancillary powers found at s.44 CIA. Officers are required to be fully conversant with the powers conferred under a search warrant within the relevant Act prior to executing the search warrant.

sections.t: Other — Adjacent Premises (Senior Officer Order) — s.44(2)/(3) CIA
sections.h:
Adjacent Premises – Senior Officer Order

s.44(2) CIA search warrant ancillary powers makes provision for police to enter but not search a place near the target place if an officer reasonably suspects it is necessary to do so in order to prevent a thing from being concealed or disturbed, prevent a person fleeing a target place, or to protect the safety of any person, including the officer, who is near the target place.

s.44(3) CIA states that this power cannot be exercised without senior officer approval. Senior Officer orders are located here.

sections.t: Annexure One — Search Warrant Guide (ready reckoner with caution and statement scripts)
sections.h:
ANNEXURE ONE – SEARCH WARRANT GUIDE

This guide is not comprehensive and is intended as a ready reckoner for Members in Charge (MIC) of search warrants to prompt their memories relating to key processes during a search warrant. Officers using this guide are to refer to the Investigative Standards Unit Search Warrant Procedures manual for further information.

Thoroughly brief all officers on all aspects of the search warrant to be conducted, point and type of entry, known suspects, items of interest, roles and responsibilities. See Operational Order. Check correct address and particulars and ensure MIC is in possession of signed warrant.

COMMENCEMENT OF SEARCH WARRANT

Knock and Announce Tactical Forced Entry

• Activate BWC on approach.

• Identify yourself and advise occupier of BWC.

• Inform the occupier of intent to enter the

place.

• Give occupier copy of warrant and inform

the occupier of the reason and the statutory

authority for the entry.

• Give the occupier an opportunity to give

consent to the place being entered.

• Secure the premises and assemble those

present in a suitable area.

• Conduct Forced Entry Risk Assessment during planning

phase prior to entry.

• Consider Senior Officer Orders for neighbouring

properties.

• Activate BWC on approach.

• Enter premises as per OSSTU Method of Entry

procedures.

• Detain all parties and assemble in a suitable area.

As soon as practicable after the place is entered:

• Identify yourself

• Give occupier a copy of search warrant

• If the entry was under some other statutory authority,

inform the occupier of the reason, and the statutory

authority, for the entry.

Provide Caution and CIA Rights to all occupants and advise each person of their status.

Searching Persons for Security Risk Items

• Basic search with camera on, or

• Strip search all parties one at a time whilst camera is still on (not in the view of the camera).

INTRODUCTION

“I advise you the camera is now recording everything we say and do. The time is_________, the date is __________, and we are at “ “

“I am [Member in Charge] from the [ Business Unit ]. The other officers and I are here to execute a Search Warrant pursuant to the Act. You have been provided a copy of the search warrant and as stated on the warrant, we are looking for certain items in connection with [ specify offence details ].” Read out list of items.

Get all persons present to state their full name, date of birth and address.

Advise who (if any) is under arrest. Advise who (if any) is being detained for the purposes of a basic search or strip search. Give arrested suspect rights or accompanying person’s rights to all parties. If an accompanying person may become a suspect in the future, also provide s.137 and s.138 rights to the person.Arrested Suspects Rights s.137/138 CIA Notified Of Afforded

• Inform of the offence for which they have

been arrested.

• Caution before interviewing.

• Reasonable opportunity to communicate with

a lawyer.

• Reasonable opportunity to communicate with

a friend or family member to advise them of

their whereabouts.

• Any necessary medical treatment

• Reasonable degree of privacy from mass media

• If they are unable to understand spoken English

sufficiently, not to be interviewed until the services of an interpreter are available.

Accompanying Persons Rights s.28CIA

• They are not under arrest

• They do not have to accompany officers

• If they do accompany officers they are free to leave at any time unless they are subsequently arrested.

If there is any uncertainty whether the accompanying person may become a suspect, provide all of their rights at s.137 and s.138 CIA, with the exception of telling them they are under arrest.

CAUTION ALL PERSONS PRESENT

“You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so. Anything you do say or do will be recorded and may be given in evidence.”

Ensure each occupant adequately details their understanding of the caution and their knowledge;

• They do not have to answer any questions;

• If they do respond to questions their answers will be recorded; and

• The recording may be used as evidence in court.

CONDUCT A PAN OF THE HOUSE/PRELIMINARY QUESTIONING

“We will now conduct a pan of the house to show the condition it is in. Please follow the camera operator to identify each room.” Security Officer to accompany. Record any damage made on entry.

“Before we start searching are there any large amounts of money or valuables you wish to secure?”

“Are there any items that may be a risk to officers conducing the search such as syringes, weapons, chemicals or anything else?”

“Are there any drugs or items listed on the search warrant you wish to declare?”

“We are going to commence our search and the camera will be turned off. If anything of interest is found the camera will be turned back on and you may be asked questions in relation to anything found.”

“The time is _____ and we’re going to turn off the camera.”

Ensure the occupant can clearly view each area being searched. RECOMMENCING SEARCH WARRANT RECORDINGS “The time is the date is and we are still at . “

Provide a short commentary on what occurred whilst the camera was switched off and invite the occupier to comment.

“I remind you that you are still under caution and don’t have to say anything unless you wish to do so. Your rights given to you earlier are ongoing and can be exercised at any time”

CONCLUSION OF SEARCH WARRANT

“The time is ___________, The date is______________ and we are at______________________.

We have completed the search. I’ll hand you over to the physical material officer who will go through the seized items.”

PHYSICAL MATERIAL OFFICER (Recorded on Camera)

• Go through exhibits log (show exhibit log number to camera), name what items have been seized.

• Sign exhibit log and give the occupier the opportunity to sign the exhibit log.

• Give the occupier their copy of the exhibit log.

CONDUCT A FINAL SCAN OF THE HOUSE

“We will now conduct a final pan of the house to show what condition it is in.” Identify if any damage was made whilst searching.

“Have Police caused any damage to your premises today? Did Police damage anything whilst entering? Did Police damage anything whilst searching?”

PRIOR TO FINISHING RECORDING ASK ANY SUSPECT WHO HAS BEEN QUESTIONED “Has everything you’ve said on camera been of your own free will? Have police made you any promises or threatened you in any way?”

“Do you have any complaints about how the search warrant was conducted or about how you have been treated by Police today?”

“Is there anything else you wish to say in relation to this or any other matter?”

“A formal assessment of the evidence and/or further inquiries will now be undertaken and a decision made in relation to what further action we will take. I will advise you of the outcome once that decision has been made.”

“If you are charged with an offence in relation to this search warrant a copy of this recording can be made available to you or your legal representative. If you want a copy of this recording you must provide Police a written request and the recording will be made available to you within 14 days.”

“That completes the search warrant. The time is __________ on .

Turn off camera. MIC to endorse the occupier’s copy of the search warrant (previously given) and advise who (if any) is under arrest.

sections.t: Annexure Two — Search Warrant Quick Guide (key evidentiary benchmarks)
sections.h:
ANNEXURE TWO - SEARCH WARRANT QUICK

GUIDE

Officers using this quick guide are to refer to the Investigative Standards Search Warrant Manual. This guide is not comprehensive and is intended as a ready reckoner for members in charge of searches, to refresh their memories relating to key evidentiary benchmarks during a search warrant. This document omits key considerations found in the manual.

Immediately Prior to Entry:

• The IO is to have the signed Search Warrant in their physical possession

• Check correct address / time / particulars.

Occupiers Consent:

• BWC activated – occupant advised

• Provide CIA occupier’s rights

• Give a copy of search warrant to the occupant

• Member in charge will coordinate BW activation and deactivation (to buffering mode).

Evidence Collection:

• Introduction of member in charge. Time, date, location (recorded on camera)

• Name, address and DOB of occupants

• CIA Arrested Suspect / Accompanying Person Rights

• Check of Understanding

• Cash or valuables / items posing a risk of harm

• Audiovisual pan of premises

• Question to Occupant – are the items listed in the warrant here?

Search:

• Advise occupant of search process

• Question to Occupant – do you have any questions?

• Systematic search – room by room

• Locate item - activate BWC, recording the last 30 secs

• Member in charge coordinate camera operator to commence audiovisual recording

and deactivation (buffering mode) of searching officer’s BWC

• Member in charge to revisit caution, rights and check of understanding when

audiovisual device is turned back on

• Relevant investigative interviewing questions*.

Search Complete:

• Physical Material Officer to provide copy of exhibits register

• Record and address any complaints

• Question to Occupant – any questions?

• Pan of premises

• Time and date - announce time and completion of warrant.

*Note – this does not necessarily negate the need for audiovisual record of interview upon return to police station.
